data.title: Brown-Forman reaffirms low single-digit organic net sales decline outlook as U.S. distributor transition, innovation drive performance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Brown-Forman Corporation (BF.B) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO Lawson Whiting highlighted that the operating environment remains challenging with cyclical pressures in developed markets—"cyclical pressures related to ongoing macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainties continued to negatively impact consumer confidence and reduced discretionary spending in the U.S. and many developed international markets." He pointed to resilience in emerging markets, noting "Mexico and Brazil continued to deliver strong double-digit growth."
  • Whiting emphasized that "New Mix, the world's first tequila-based RTD delivered very strong double-digit growth as the brand continued to lead the category and gain share in Mexico." Jack Daniel's Tennessee Apple and Jack Daniel's Tennessee Whiskey led growth in Brazil.
  • In the U.K., "Jack Daniel's Tennessee Whiskey continues to gain market share" despite a challenging environment. The U.S. saw flat organic net sales, but performance "continues to be ahead of our depletion-based results as well as takeaway trends driven by the powerful combination of our U.S. distributor changes and the launch of Jack Daniel's Tennessee Blackberry."
  • The phased international launch of Jack Daniel's Tennessee Blackberry was described as exceeding expectations: "Blackberry is getting wonderful feedback and buzz from distributors, retailers, consumers, both new and existing and the media."
  • Whiting addressed two unique headwinds: "Organic net sales for used barrels decreased by more than 60%... Canada's organic net sales also declined over 60%." Despite these, "we remain positioned to achieve our full year guidance."
  • Whiting provided an update on CFO succession: "Our recruitment process for her successor is well underway... we anticipate that the process may extend into early next calendar year."
  • CFO Leanne Cunningham stated, "reported gross profit decreased 4%, resulting in a reported gross margin of 59.5%." She added, "free cash flow... increased by $179 million to $236 million, reflecting strong operating cash flow generation and lower capital expenditure needs."
  • Cunningham highlighted capital allocation actions: "On November 19, 2025, our Board of Directors approved a 2% increase in the quarterly cash dividend, marking 42 consecutive years of dividend increases."

Outlook

  • The company reaffirmed its full-year fiscal 2026 outlook: "We continue to expect a low single-digit decline in organic net sales, and reported gross margin expansion as we believe the benefit from A&D will more than offset the headwinds from negative price/mix and higher costs."
  • Management continues to assume "no change to the current tariff impact, direct and indirect on our products."
  • "We are updating our estimated capital expenditures outlook from a range of $125 million to $135 million to a range of $110 million to $120 million for the full year."

Financial Results

  • Cunningham reported, "reported gross profit decreased 4%, resulting in a reported gross margin of 59.5%."
  • "In total, reported operating income decreased 9% and organic operating income decreased 4% in the first half of fiscal 2026."
  • A 13% diluted earnings per share decrease to $0.83 per share was reported.
  • "Capital expenditures decreased by $16 million compared to the year ago period."
  • "We grew cash flows from operations by $163 million to $292 million."
  • "As of October 31, 2025, we have repurchased $99 million of our outstanding shares of Class A and Class B common stock."

Q&A

  • Lauren Lieberman, Barclays: Asked about the impact of new distribution partners during the holiday season and the level of caution among new distributor partners. Whiting responded, "California was the first... while the transition took longer than we expected, it is now moving and improving quickly... The other 13 markets went live on August 1... the teams are in place. Everything is moving pretty much as planned." He added, "The RTD category is really the only category gaining share."
  • Peter Grom, UBS: Inquired about category deceleration and holiday expectations. Whiting said, "total distilled spirits has weakened in the last 6 months or so, not dramatically, but it has... our expectations for this holiday season are—we're trying to be optimistic, but at the same time, we are looking and seeing some of the macro trends that are tough."
  • Nadine Sarwat, Bernstein: Asked about the U.S. net sales and pricing strategy. Cunningham explained, "it's really how we are seeing the flow-through of innovation with the impact of Jack Daniel's Tennessee Blackberry launch... As we go through the rest of the fiscal year, we expect those to narrow." Whiting added, "Brown-Forman's pricing across the entire portfolio is down 0.3%."
  • Robert Ottenstein, Evercore ISI: Queried about trade down trends and advertising spend. Whiting clarified, "$100 plus is down 18%... from $10 to $30... they're all down around 4%." Cunningham stated, "we are continuing to focus on investing behind our brands, and we're focusing on maintaining and growing our share of voice."
  • Drew Levine, JPMorgan: Requested an update on the Jack Daniel's family and Blackberry's impact. Whiting said, "the cannibalization has been minimal. We do recognize every time you do a new one that you're going to turn up that a little bit."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on risks from consumer weakness, trade down, and distributor transitions, often with slightly negative or cautious tones, as seen in questions about category deceleration and price strategy.
  • Management maintained a slightly positive to neutral tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing resilience and confidence—"I remain fully confident in the potential of Brown-Forman, our brands and our people." In Q&A, management occasionally became defensive or cautious, particularly regarding trade down and market headwinds.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remained cautious, while management tone shifted slightly more positive as transitions stabilized and innovation delivered early results.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained consistent, reaffirming a low single-digit organic net sales decline and gross margin expansion.
  • Strategic focus remained on innovation and distributor transitions. The current quarter featured more detailed updates on phased international launches and workforce restructuring.
  • Key metric changes included a continued decrease in operating income, gross profit, and EPS, but improved cash flow and reduced capital expenditures.
  • Management confidence slightly increased, with more emphasis on early positive impacts from innovation and distributor transitions. Analysts maintained a cautious focus on consumer and category trends.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management outlined persistent headwinds from lower used barrel sales and ongoing trade disputes with Canada, both cited as substantial negative impacts to top-line performance.
  • Inflation and lower production volumes continue to pressure costs.
  • Analysts continued to raise concerns about consumer weakness, trade down, and the pace of inventory normalization.

Final Takeaway

Brown-Forman's second quarter of fiscal 2026 demonstrated resilience in emerging markets and the global travel retail channel, while developed markets and the U.S. faced cyclical consumer headwinds. Management reaffirmed its outlook for a low single-digit decline in organic net sales and reported gross margin expansion, supported by innovation, successful distributor transitions, and disciplined capital allocation. While unique headwinds from used barrel sales and Canadian trade restrictions weighed on results, the company remains focused on executing its strategy and driving long-term brand growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wiley narrows revenue outlook to low single digits for 2026 while accelerating AI initiatives and cost discipline
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: John Wiley & Sons, Inc. (WLY) Q2 2026

Management View

  • Matthew Kissner, President and CEO, reported a mixed quarter: "We saw a mixed revenue picture with strong growth in Research and good momentum in AI, offset by market challenges in our Learning segment." He emphasized the company's AI growth strategy and highlighted that "Research Publishing delivered strong 7% growth on worldwide demand to publish, read and license." Kissner also noted, "We executed another AI licensing project for an existing LLM customer this quarter, putting us close to $100 million of AI training revenue in less than 2 years."
  • Kissner stated, "We continue to advance our strategic partnerships with AWS, Anthropic and Perplexity and added Mistral AI during the quarter." He detailed cost controls and capital allocation, saying, "Our Q2 adjusted operating margin was up 250 basis points to 18.8%. We increased our share repurchases by 69% this quarter to $21 million."
  • Addressing Learning segment headwinds, Kissner cited "a significant change in inventory management from Amazon," "soft" consumer spending, and "enrollment challenges in select Wiley disciplines, namely computer science, down 8% in the fall semester." He added that "cost actions will help offset any top line impact."
  • Kissner described the research segment as resilient and a "long-term tailwind and a growth engine," citing high institutional retention and growth in open access.
  • Craig Albright, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "Adjusted EBITDA grew 8% and adjusted operating margin expanded 250 basis points to 18.8%."
  • Albright highlighted that "Research delivered 5% growth and a 220 basis point improvement in EBITDA margin to 33.5%." He also said, "Learning was down 11% in the quarter, driven primarily by headwinds in professional and academic as well as prior year AI revenue of $4 million."

Outlook

  • Albright reaffirmed, "We're reaffirming guidance for adjusted EBITDA margin, adjusted EPS and free cash flow, while narrowing our revenue outlook to the lower end of the range."
  • He stated, "Revenue growth is now expected to be in the low single digits, down from our prior range of low to mid-single digits."
  • Albright projected, "We expect AI revenue to be moderately ahead of last year's $40 million. Adjusted EBITDA margin of 25.5% to 26.5%, up from 24% last year; adjusted EPS of $3.90 to $4.35, up from $3.64 last year; and free cash flow of approximately $200 million."
  • He added, "We anticipate Q3 will be lighter than typical due to the timing of AI project revenue, which creates a year-over-year headwind of approximately $9 million in Research. Growth is weighted to Q4."

Financial Results

  • Albright reported, "Free cash flow was a use of $108 million, a 17% or $22 million improvement from prior year. CapEx was $31 million compared to $36 million in the prior year."
  • He noted, "Share repurchases were up 69% to $21 million or $35 million year-to-date compared to $25 million in the prior year period."
  • Albright pointed out, "Net debt-to-EBITDA was 2.0x on a trailing 12-month basis, down from 2.2x in the prior year."
  • The company acquired the journal Nanophotonics, strengthening its physics portfolio.

Q&A

  • Dan Moore, CJS Securities, Inc.: "Research revenue grew 5% ex-currency... Does that feel like the right place to be?...could be some incremental upside...just given the strong and continuing high double-digit growth that we've seen in article submission over the past few quarters?" Matthew Kissner responded, "We think we're going to be growing at the top of the market growth, if not, I think there's the potential to outperform given our strong article growth."
  • Moore: "Maybe a little bit more color around the $6 million in revenue that you booked during the quarter. It sounds like it was with an existing LLM customer. Talk about the pipeline..." Craig Albright replied, "The $6 million deal...was majority Wiley content. It was a repeat customer...We have also talked about the fact that this is a bit lumpy, that it's hard to predict exactly when the deals come in."
  • Moore: "Learning, obviously, remains challenged. Maybe your best guess for how much of the decline is due to sort of end market demand versus inventory management at large retailers, Amazon specifically..." Kissner answered, "Everybody is seeing the impact of this...change in inventory strategy just because of the importance of Amazon in this business...ordering pattern is starting to normalize."
  • Moore: "You stepped up the pace of buybacks during the quarter...how are you sort of weighing the accelerated buybacks versus delevering as we look at the back half of the year?" Albright replied, "We have a very disciplined approach to capital allocation...we could continue to use some of that towards share buybacks...we continue to be opportunistic."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst questions were neutral with a focus on revenue growth, AI pipeline, Learning headwinds, and capital allocation, reflecting cautious interest and some concern on Learning.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, especially regarding research segment growth and AI initiatives, using phrases like "we are fully confident in our Research core and excited about the expanding AI opportunities in front of us."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence level on AI and cost discipline remained high, while addressing Learning with more candor about headwinds.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for adjusted EBITDA margin, EPS, and free cash flow was reaffirmed, but the revenue outlook was narrowed from "low to mid-single digits" in Q1 to "low single digits" in Q2.
  • AI revenue is expected to be "moderately ahead" of last year’s $40 million, consistent with Q1’s outlook.
  • Share repurchases accelerated in Q2 compared to Q1.
  • Learning segment headwinds were discussed in greater detail, with specific mention of Amazon’s inventory changes, which were not as prominent in Q1.
  • Management’s tone on research remained confident, with more explicit discussion on AI licensing and partnerships, while Learning was discussed as cyclical rather than structural.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted more toward the details of AI licensing deals and the breakdown of Learning segment challenges.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "a significant change in inventory management from Amazon," "soft" consumer spending, and "enrollment challenges in select Wiley disciplines, namely computer science, down 8% in the fall semester" as headwinds for Learning.
  • Corporate spending and hiring softness affected Learning’s assessments business.
  • Management is prioritizing digital offerings, pricing strategies, and targeted marketing campaigns as mitigation strategies, with cost actions to help offset top line impact.

Final Takeaway

Wiley’s Q2 2026 call emphasized strong growth and margin expansion in the Research segment, supported by robust demand and momentum in AI licensing, while the Learning segment faced industry and macroeconomic headwinds. The company reaffirmed profit and cash flow guidance, narrowed its revenue outlook, and highlighted disciplined capital allocation with increased share repurchases. Management indicated confidence in the resilience of the research business, ongoing AI innovation, and its ability to navigate Learning challenges through targeted actions and cost discipline.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: PepsiCo close to settlement with activist investor Elliott - WSJ
data.description:
Prominent activist investor Elliott Investment Management is nearing a settlement agreement with PepsiCo (PEP) after taking a large stake in the beverage and snacks behemoth in September.

Details of the settlement weren't known, though an announcement could happen soon, according to a WSJ report on Thursday, which cited people familiar with the matter.

A potential settlement comes after Elliott  took a $4 billion stake in PepsiCo (PEP) in September and pushed for engagement with the company's board. Elliott wants PepsiCo to evaluate refranchising its bottling network, and divesting non-core and underperforming assets.

PepsiCo (PEP) in October announced that its chief financial officer was retiring and being replaced by former Walmart U.S. CFO Steve Schmitt.

data.title: CROs fall as FDA head Makary says drug approvals will require fewer trials
data.description:
• Contract research organizations such as Charles River Laboratories (CRL), ICON (ICLR), and IQVIA (IQV), are trading in the red on Thursday after FDA Commissioner Marty Makary said that pharmas will only need to submit results from one pivotal trial for most new drug approval applications.
  • "You can achieve the same statistical power with one trial as you would with two trials when it's designed and controlled appropriately," he told STAT. 
  • Medpace (MEDP) is the hardest hit CRO so far , down ~4%, followed by IQVIA and Fortrea (FTRE), both off ~2%.

data.title: Knife River rated Neutral in new coverage by J.P. Morgan; 'show me' margin story
data.description:
J.P. Morgan analyst Adrian Huerta on Thursday initiated coverage of Knife River (KNF) with a Neutral rating. The firm describes Knife River (KNF) as a vertically integrated aggregates and construction materials supplier with a distinctive presence across 14 Western and Central U.S. states, markets where it often holds the No. 1 share and faces limited direct competition from national players.

Knife River’s (KNF) heavy exposure to public infrastructure work (80% to 87% of its contracting backlog and about half of materials revenue tied to government-funded projects) provides above-average revenue visibility, Huerta wrote. Management’s emphasis on dynamic pricing, operational programs such as EDGE and PIT Crews, and a strategy of aggregates-led acquisitions could support long-term improvement in ebitda margins, which the company ultimately aims to lift above 20%.

Still, J.P. Morgan sees limited visibility in the near and medium term. Huerta points to lower free cash flow conversion versus peers, the absence of plans to return capital to shareholders, and the need to prove out early-stage margin initiatives as reasons to remain on the sidelines, even as the balance of risks trends positive if execution improves.

The firm notes that Knife River’s (KNF) geographic footprint, situated in higher-growth regions but still largely mid-tier markets, may constrain how far its pricing power can expand. Dynamic pricing could help close the roughly 20% price gap between the company and larger national aggregates competitors, but structural limits in these markets may temper the gains.

Knife River’s (KNF) vertical integration allows it to capture more ebitda dollars by controlling materials, contracting, and downstream project execution. But its free cash flow conversion rate of 25% to 30% trails national peers, which are closer to 50%. According to Huerta, achieving a 20% ebitda margin, compared with J.P. Morgan’s forecast of 15.6% for 2025, would lift FCF conversion toward 45%, and reducing maintenance capex to 5% of revenue (from roughly 6% today) could add another five percentage points.

J.P. Morgan set a December 2026 price target of $83, implying roughly 11% upside and valuing the company at 9.4 times forward enterprise value to ebitda, a modest discount to peer Eagle Materials (EXP).

data.title: Spot crypto can now trade on CFTC-registered exchanges
data.description:
U.S.-listed spot crypto products will start trading for the first time on registered futures exchanges, the Commodity Futures Trading Commission said on Thursday, a move that signals digital assets are being pulled deeper into traditional markets.

“Recent events on offshore exchanges have shown us how essential it is for Americans to have more choice and access to safe, regulated U.S. markets," CFTC Acting Chairman Caroline Pham said in a statement.

The move advances the Trump administration's push to make the U.S. a global hub for crypto, tracking recommendations from the President's Working Group on Digital Assets, which earlier in the year outlined a crypto agenda for regulators.

In September, the CFTC launched an initiative inviting interest stakeholders to submit feedback on the use of tokenized collateral, including stablecoins, in derivatives markets.

data.title: Semiconductor billings surge in October with DRAM up 90%: Wells Fargo
data.description:
Semiconductor billings surged 33% year over year to total $71.3B in October, according to Wells Fargo, citing the most recent data from the Semiconductor Industry Association.

Dynamic random access memory, or DRAM, accounted for much of the jump as DRAM billings rocketed 90% year over year to reach $12.82B. The most prolific producers of DRAM include Micron Technology (MU), Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) and SK Hynix. NAND billings increased 13% year over year to total $5.13B.

"We would note that SIA's implied NAND capacity ship estimates were materially revised for September—thus implying declines in $/GB (i.e., -16% m/m in Oct?), which we question given the current market dynamics," said Wells Fargo analysts, led by Aaron Rakers, in an industry note.

Microprocessor billings totaled $5.98B in October, which was a year-over-year increase of 16%. However, despite the jump in billings, the total number of MPUs shipped declined 4% year over year.

Analog billings were even stronger, increasing by 18% year over year to total $7.93B. Units shipped climbed 11% year over year.

Microcontroller billings also increased 18% year over year to $1.88B, with shipments up 21%. MOSFET power billings gained 19% year over year to $1.02B.

Semiconductor stocks were demonstrating mixed action during Thursday trading. Micron was down 3%, while Nvidia (NVDA) had ticked up 2%. Intel (INTC) had dropped 6%, AMD (AMD) had inched down 0.5% and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) had declined 1%. Texas Instruments (TXN) had slipped 1% and Analog Devices (ADI) was up 0.2%.

data.title: U.S. stocks see modest gains after trade struggle due to rate cuts anticipation
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages shyly held onto gains on Thursday as investors assessed Meta Platforms' (META) rise after Mark Zuckerberg is reportedly cutting Metaverse spending, and jobless claims came lower than expected.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.1% in afternoon trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.2%, and the Dow (DJI) was +0.1%.

Meta Platforms (META) -- last +4% -- rose as Bloomberg reported that Mark Zuckerberg said that the company is looking at cutting Metaverse efforts by up to 30%.

On the economic side, the initial jobless claims retreated to below 200K and were better than expected. U.S.-based employers announced 71.32K job cuts in November, down 53% from the 153.07K announced in October.

“Initial claims collapsed last week to a three-year low. The Bureau of Labor Statistics, which compiles this report, did not note any special factors. So, as I highlighted in red all year, the less-than-5-day workweeks distort this series,” said Jim Bianco, president and macro strategist at Bianco Research.

“Last week was Thanksgiving. Previously, these short workweeks resulted in a spike in claims, but Thanksgiving week saw a collapse. Even if you adjust for this week for Thanksgiving, the data still doesn't suggest the Fed should cut rates next week,” he added.

The Federal Reserve will announce its interest rate decision next week. Odds for a 25-basis-point cut are at 87%, according to the CME FedWatch Tool.

In other news, President Donald Trump's aides were reportedly in talks about appointing Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent as the top White House economic adviser, in addition to his current role, if Kevin Hassett is picked as the next Federal Reserve chair.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 3 basis points to 4.10%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 4 basis points to 3.54%.

data.title: VinFast plans to expand capacity in India for electric bus and electric scooter production
data.description:
VinFast Auto Ltd. (VFS) announced on Thursday that it struck a deal with the government of Tamil Nadu in India to allocate approximately 200 hectares of land in the SIPCOT Industrial Park in Thoothukudi to support the development of the company’s existing facility. The electric vehicle maker said the milestone provides an opportunity for it to broaden its product portfolio from electric cars to electric buses, e-scooters, and charging infrastructure.

Under the terms of the memorandum of understanding, the government of Tamil Nadu will allocate approximately 500 acres of land adjacent to VinFast’s (VFS) existing facility in Thoothukudi. The state will also provide support in securing required permits and in establishing essential infrastructure connections such as electricity, water, internal road access, drainage, and waste management.

As part of a second phase of its existing $2 billion commitment, VinFast (VFS) will invest $500 million in Thoothukudi to develop new dedicated workshops and production lines for electric buses and e-scooters, covering manufacturing, assembly, testing, and other related operations.

The existing Thoothukudi facility covers 400 acres (160 hectares) and is equipped with internationally standardized production lines. It has an initial annual capacity of 50,000 electric vehicles and is being expanded to 150,000 units. 

"The proposed expansion of the Tamil Nadu plant will enable us to broaden our product lineup in India, from electric cars to electric buses and e-scooters, allowing us to meet a wider range of customer needs," highlighted Vingroup Asia CEO Pham Sanh Chau.

Shares of VinFast (VFS) are up more than 4% this week but still show double-digit year-to-date decline.

data.title: Top retail stocks by YTD performance as Costco flops
data.description:
In the face of consumer spending concerns, government shutdowns, and reduced SNAP payments, certain retail stocks have demonstrated remarkable resilience.

We present the best performing retail stocks as Costco (COST) fails to deliver gains year-to-date, even after another consistent month of comparable sales growth in November. The company has also recently been on the spotlight after suing the U.S. government to receive a full refund of tariffs it already paid amid the expected Supreme Court rule on whether President Donald Trump's levies are illegal.

The big-box warehouse club retail store is down 8.5% over the last 52 weeks and down 2.5% year-to-date, despite its solid November sales figures of $23.64 billion (up 8.1% year-over-year) with comparable sales rising 6.9%.

This stock screen highlights the retail sector’s top performers, showcasing companies across various segments including apparel, automotive, and broadline retail.

Each stock is presented with its corresponding Seeking Alpha Quant Rating where available, providing additional context beyond pure performance metrics. This comprehensive view helps identify not just which stocks have performed well historically, but which might be positioned for continued strength according to analyst evaluations.

Here are the top U.S. retail companies, ranked by year-to-date performance:
